question: If I fail a drug test, what happens to my employment?
answer: According to our Drug and Alcohol Testing Policy, Section: Workplace Conduct, if you test positive, you will be immediately removed from duty.
contexts:

Any employee who tests positive will be immediately removed from duty.

All drug-testing information will be maintained in separate confidential records.

Pre-employment Testing: Every job applicant will be required to take and pass a drug and/or alcohol test before
he or she may officially be hired by . Each applicant will be notified that a drug and/or alcohol test is required as
part of the interview process and that any and all job offers are contingent upon successfully passing a drug
and/or alcohol test.

ground_truth: Employees who test positive or refuse testing are subject to disciplinary action up to and including termination and may be removed from duty until rehabilitation and negative retest requirements are met. The EAP may be available, but participation does not excuse policy violations.

ground_truth: No. Firearms and other lethal weapons are prohibited on company property, in vehicles used for company business, in company parking facilities, and at work-related functions, including for employees licensed to carry weapons, except when weapons are required for specific job duties.

question: What insurance coverage do I need to use my personal car for business travel?
answer:
According to our Business Expense Reimbursement Policy, Section: General Practices, any damages, repair costs or maintenance costs incurred by an employee in the use of their privately owned vehicle in conjunction with company business is the sole responsibility of the employee.

However, to be eligible to use a personal automobile for business-related travel, it must be covered by a current insurance policy with limits not less than $100,000 for bodily injury and $50,000 for property damage. 

[Source 1 | Policy: Business Expense Reimbursement Policy | Section: General Practices]
contexts:
The use of a personal automobile for business-related travel is only authorized if the automobile is covered by a current
insurance policy with limits not less than $100,000 for bodily injury and $50,000 for property damage. Any
damages, repair costs or maintenance costs incurred by an employee in the use of their privately owned vehicle in
conjunction with company business is the sole responsibility of the employee.
Car Rentals
Employees who drive for company business must have a current, valid driver’s license and required insurance.

equal to the Internal Revenue Service optional mileage allowance for such usage. This allowance is to
compensate for the cost of gasoline, oil, depreciation and insurance. Employees who operate personal vehicles
for Company business should obtain auto liability coverage for bodily injury and property damage with a special
ground_truth: Your automobile must be covered by a current insurance policy with limits not less than $100,000 for bodily injury and $50,000 for property damage. You remain responsible for damages, repair, and maintenance costs.

question: Under what conditions can an employee be terminated immediately without warning?
answer:
According to the Disciplinary Action Policy, Section: Workplace Conduct, an employee can be terminated immediately without warning in the following situations:

- Discourtesy to a customer, vendor or the general public resulting in a complaint or loss of good will (Source 1)
- Refusal or failure to follow directions from management; insubordination (Source 1)
- Breach of confidentiality relating to employer, employee, customer or vendor information (Source 1)
- During the orientation period, if the employee's job performance is found to be unsatisfactory by their supervisor at any time during the first 90 days of employment (Source 3)
- If the employee tests positive for a prohibited substance during a drug and alcohol test (Source 5)
- If the employee brings a weapon into the workplace (Source 4)

Additionally, according to the Disciplinary Action Policy, Section: Workplace Conduct, reserves the right to take any disciplinary action, including termination, at any time, and prior warning is not a requirement for termination (Source 2).
contexts:
In addition to those situations discussed elsewhere in this handbook, listed below are some examples where immediate
termination could result. This list is general in nature and is not intended to be all inclusive:
o
Discourtesy to a customer, vendor or the general public resulting in a complaint or loss of good will
o
Refusal or failure to follow directions from management; insubordination
o
Breach of confidentiality relating to employer, employee, customer or vendor information

Disciplinary actions may entail verbal, written and final warnings, suspensions and termination. Not all of these actions
may be followed in all instances. reserves the right to exercise discretion in discipline. Prior warning is not a requirement
for termination. All disciplinary actions will be documented; documentation will be placed in personnel files.
reserves the right to take any disciplinary action the company considers appropriate, including termination, at any time.

her job responsibilities, and to determine if he or she is satisfied with the position. If the employee’s job performance is
found to be unsatisfactory by his or her supervisor at any time during the first 90 days of employment, the employment
will be terminated.
All new employees will receive a confidential performance evaluation from their supervisor at the end of the orientation
period.
ground_truth: Prior warning is not always required for termination. Examples where immediate termination may result include insubordination, dishonesty, breach of confidentiality, destruction of property, fighting, serious policy violations, serious conduct or performance issues, or failing a drug or alcohol test.

question: When is an employee eligible for FMLA leave?
answer:
According to our Federal Family and Medical Leave Policy (Source 3 | Section: Time Away From Work), an employee must meet the following eligibility requirements:

1. Have been employed by the Company for at least 12 months.
2. Have worked at least 1,250 hours in the 12 months immediately preceding the beginning of the leave.
3. Work in an office or work site where 50 or more employees are employed within 75 miles of that office or work site.

If an employee meets these requirements, they are eligible for FMLA leave.
contexts:
[EMPLOYER: If the employer is covered and the employee is eligible for FMLA, those rights should be considered before
this policy.]
Within the First Year of Employment
An employee experiencing a serious medical condition within his or her first year of employment may request a leave of
absence. A leave of up to [number of weeks] weeks within the first 12 months of service may be provided when the
employee needs to be out of work for at least five consecutive workdays.

complies with all federal and state family and leave laws. However, when these laws do not apply or an employee does
not meet the eligibility requirements, the company will consider an employee’s request for non-FMLA medical leave.
This leave may provide up to [maximum leave period] in a 12-month period, unless otherwise required by law. Each
leave request will be considered on an individual basis.

Eligibility
To be eligible for leave, you must have been employed by the Company for at least 12 months. In the 12 months
immediately preceding the beginning of the leave, you must also have worked at least 1,250 hours to qualify for federal
FMLA. In addition, you must work in an office or work site where 50 or more employees are employed within 75 miles of
that office or work site.
Amount of Leave Available
ground_truth: After at least 12 months of employment, at least 1,250 hours worked in the 12 months before leave begins, and work at a site with 50 or more employees within 75 miles (federal FMLA eligibility).

question: Under what circumstances is overtime mandatory?
answer: I don't have enough information in our HR policies to answer that question.
contexts:
must compensate all hourly, non-exempt employees one-and-a-half times their regular wage rate for all hours worked
in excess of 40 hours each week.
The company’s workweek begins on 5:00 PM on Friday and ends on 9:00 AM on Monday.
At times, employees will be asked to work overtime to complete necessary work tasks. The employee’s supervisor will
notify the employee as early as possible regarding scheduling needs.

Employees are classified as either exempt or non-exempt for pay administration purposes, as determined by the federal
Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA).
The definitions of the worker classification categories can be summarized as follows:
Exempt—Employees who meet any of the FLSA’s exemption standards, including managerial, supervisory, professional,
sales or administrative employees.
Non-exempt—Employees whose positions do not meet the FLSA exemption standards. Overtime work is prohibited

Employees who want to work more than 40 hours during a workweek must receive written authorization from their
supervisor before working overtime.
(EMPLOYER NOTE: State wage and hour laws differ—check with your legal advisor.)
ground_truth: The handbook states employees may be asked to work overtime to complete necessary tasks and supervisors should give notice as early as possible. Working more than 40 hours in a workweek requires written supervisor authorization for non-exempt employees; mandatory overtime with discipline for refusal is not spelled out the way the old summary suggested.

question: What happens if an employee doesn't return from leave by the expected date?
answer:
If an employee doesn't return from leave by the expected date, according to the Non-FMLA Leave Policy | Section: Time Away From Work (Source 3), employment ends effective the last day of the approved leave. Exceptions will be made if continued leave is granted as an accommodation under the Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA). Other exemptions may also be granted at management’s discretion. Each situation will be reviewed on an individual basis.

Additionally, according to the Personal Leave Policy | Section: Time Away From Work (Source 1), the employee will receive written notice from Human Resources as soon as the determination has been made and no later than the employee’s scheduled return date.
contexts:
or her return. In the event this cannot be done, the employee will receive written notice from Human Resources as soon
as the determination has been made and no later than the employee’s scheduled return date.
Returning to Work
Employees are expected to be able to return to work by the end of their approved leave. If an employee on leave plans
to return to work sooner than scheduled, the employee must notify his or her supervisor within two business days in
advance of reporting to work.

the employee to return to work with restrictions, the supervisor will consult with Human Resources to determine if the
restrictions can be met.
If an employee is released to return to work sooner than the expected return date that was provided when he or she
requested the leave, the employee must notify his or her supervisor within two business days of receiving the release.
All return to work documentation will be kept with Human Resources.
Unable to Return to Work

Unable to Return to Work
If the employee is not medically released to return to work at the end of his or her leave, employment ends effective the
last day of the approved leave. Exceptions will be made if continued leave is granted as an accommodation under the
Americans with Disabilities Act (ADA). Other exemptions may also be granted at management’s discretion. Each situation
will be reviewed on an individual basis.
Failure to Return to Work
ground_truth: Under personal leave, failing to return as scheduled may be treated as voluntary termination or require reimbursing premiums, depending on circumstances. Generally, if you fail to report to work for three consecutive days without informing management of the planned absence, the company will assume you voluntarily resigned.

question: When can an employee access their personnel file?
answer: According to our Personnel Records Policy, Section: General Practices, employees are entitled to inspect and obtain copies of their personnel files. To obtain access to your records, contact Human Resources. (Source 1: Personnel Records Policy, Section: General Practices)

Changes must be received two weeks prior to the payday for which the change is to occur.

If the company has not been closed due to severe weather, and you arrive at work after your scheduled start time, the
time missed will be charged as either (1) personal/sick time, (2) vacation time, or (3) unpaid time, in that order. You
should always use your discretion in getting to work. attempts to accommodate individual situations by allowing the use
of personal/sick time and vacation time in these situations.
ground_truth: If paydays fall on non-workdays or holidays, employees are paid on the last workday before the regularly scheduled payday.

question: Do non-smokers get a discount on health insurance?
answer:
Yes, non-smokers are eligible for a 5 percent discount on the overall cost of their health insurance premium annually. 

[Source 2 | Policy: Smoke-free Incentive Policy | Section: General Practices]
contexts:
portion of the premium. Employees will have an opportunity to qualify for the non-smoking discount at least once per
year.
The company is committed to helping employees achieve their best health. If an employee thinks that he or she might
be unable to meet the non-smoking standard, the employee might qualify for an opportunity to earn the same discount
by different means. An employee should contact Human Resources and Human Resources will work with the employee

Because VanaciPrime values the health of its employees, it is important to encourage and reward healthy habits. All employees have
the opportunity to benefit from this.
Employees who are non-smokers are eligible for a 5 percent discount on the overall cost of their health
insurance premium annually. The company will pay this percentage of the costs, in addition to the regular employer-paid

complies with the Consolidated Omnibus Budget Reconciliation Act (COBRA). This federal law gives covered employees
(and their dependents) who have lost health benefits the right to continue group health plans for limited periods of time
under certain circumstances (called “qualifying events”). All administrative rules and processes as well as changes in plan
benefits and premiums apply to those on continuation coverage.
ground_truth: Yes. Non-smokers are eligible for a 5 percent discount on the overall cost of their health insurance premium annually, with at least one opportunity per year to qualify.
